elements.sent-2411-001: Trajectory representation learning (TRL) maps trajectories to vectors that can then be used for various downstream tasks, including trajectory similarity computation, trajectory classification, and travel-time estimation.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, existing TRL methods often produce vectors that, when used in downstream tasks, yield insufficiently accurate results.
elements.sent-2411-003: A key reason is that they fail to utilize the comprehensive information encompassed by trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-004: We propose a self-supervised TRL framework, called RED, which effectively exploits multiple types of trajectory information.
elements.sent-2411-005: Overall, RED adopts the Transformer as the backbone model and masks the constituting paths in trajectories to train a masked autoencoder (MAE).
elements.sent-2411-006: In particular, RED considers the moving patterns of trajectories by employing a Road-aware masking strategy that retains key paths of trajectories during masking, thereby preserving crucial information of the trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-007: RED also adopts a spatial-temporal-user joint Embedding scheme to encode comprehensive information when preparing the trajectories as model inputs.
elements.sent-2411-008: To conduct training, RED adopts Dual-objective task learning: the Transformer encoder predicts the next segment in a trajectory, and the Transformer decoder reconstructs the entire trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-009: RED also considers the spatial-temporal correlations of trajectories by modifying the attention mechanism of the Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-010: We compare RED with 9 state-of-the-art TRL methods for 4 downstream tasks on 3 real-world datasets, finding that RED can usually improve the accuracy of the best-performing baseline by over 5\%.

elements.sent-2411-016: With the proliferation of GPS-enabled devices (e.g., smartphones, navigators, and digital watches), large amounts of trajectories are collected, recording the movements of pedestrians or vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-017: These trajectories serve as the foundation for many applications such as traffic prediction~\cite{zhao2023traffic_prediction , urban planning~\cite{pedersen2020urban_planning , and transportation optimization~\cite{yang2021transportation_optimization .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, as sequences of timestamped locations, trajectories require specific techniques for management and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, trajectory similarity computation often relies on dynamic programming~\cite{dtw,edr , resulting in computational costs that increase quadratically with trajectory length.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Recently, trajectory representation learning (TRL), which maps each trajectory to a vectors embedding, has attracted attention as a general preprocessing technique~\cite{mao2022JCLRNT,yang2022wsccl,jiang2023start,lightpath .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The advantage is that the learned vectors can be used directly in many downstream tasks, including trajectory classification~\cite{Trajformer , travel time estimation~\cite{tte1 , and trajectory similarity computation~\cite{grlstm , with standard vector processing techniques.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: For instance, trajectory similarity can be calculated as the distance between the vectors of two trajectories, with a cost that does not increase with trajectory length.
elements.sent-2411-023: Early TRL methods~\cite{traj2vec,t2vec usually target a specific task.}
elements.sent-2411-024: For example, Traj2vec~\cite{traj2vec uses Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) to map trajectories to vectors and tailors model design for trajectory clustering task.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The problem of these methods is that their vectors do not work well when used in other downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-026: Subsequent TRL methods~\cite{trembr,pim,toast utilize self-supervised learning (SSL) due to the strong generalization capabilities of SSL.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In particular, SSL relies on a generic pre-training task to distill data information, e.g., by manually masking a portion of the trajectories and learning to recover the trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-028: For example, Trembr~\cite{trembr uses RNNs~\cite{lstm with an encoder-decoder architecture.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The encoder embeds each trajectory into a vector, and the decoder recovers the trajectory from this vector.
elements.sent-2411-031: CL conducts data augmentations to generate positive and negative samples for each trajectory, and the model is trained to make positive trajectory pairs more similar than negative pairs.
elements.sent-2411-032: For instance, START~\cite{jiang2023start features several data augmentation techniques, such as randomly masking segments in the trajectories and trimming the trajectories to get sub-trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, data augmentation techniques may not generalize across datasets because their performance depends strongly on the dataset under consideration.

elements.sent-2411-034: A trajectory encompasses multiple kinds of information, including road, user, spatial-temporal, travel, and movement, but existing TRL methods do not utilize them comprehensively, resulting in trajectory representation vectors with reduced accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-035: In particular, road information, obtained by map matching~\cite{fmm , tells the road segments each trajectory passes and is less noisy than the raw trajectory points.}
elements.sent-2411-036: User information ties each trajectory to a user and encodes user preference.
elements.sent-2411-037: Spatial information refers to the spatial locations of the road segments, and a road segment usually has multiple adjacent road segments with high transition probabilities~\cite{gts .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Temporal information captures the progress over time when moving along a trajectory and can be used to estimate travel time and analyze traffic flow~\cite{jiang2023start .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Travel semantics~\cite{toast refers to the overall travel statistics of a trajectory, such as travel time and distance.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Movement semantics~\cite{traj2vec refers to the local properties of trajectories, such as whether congestion is encountered at a location.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Table~\ref{tab:trl_arch summarizes how trajectory information is utilized by existing TRL methods.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Road information is ignored by a few methods, e.g., Traj2vec~\cite{traj2vec models trajectories as point sequences without considering the underlying road properties.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Spatial information is usually considered by using the road network.
elements.sent-2411-044: For example, Toast~\cite{toast and PIM~\cite{pim apply the graph neural network to capture the topology of the road network of a city.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Temporal information is typically used to encode model inputs, e.g., Trembr~\cite{trembr includes the timestamp of each road in a trajectory as input to its RNN.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Some methods incorporate the travel semantics into trajectories to extract global information.
elements.sent-2411-047: For instance, Toast~\cite{toast , START~\cite{jiang2023start , and LightPath~\cite{lightpath utilize the learned trajectory representations to recover raw trajectories.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Only a few methods consider the moving semantics for local information, e.g., Traj2vec and Toast use context windows to extract short sequences from trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-049: START~\cite{jiang2023start is the only existing method that exploits user information.}
elements.sent-2411-050: To go beyond existing studies, we propose RED, a self-supervised learning method that aims to improve TRL by using more comprehensive trajectory information.
elements.sent-2411-051: For overall architecture, RED adopts Transformer as the backbone model and masks the segments of path trajectories to train a masked autoencoder.
elements.sent-2411-052: Regarding trajectory information, we first consider and analyze the local driving pattern of road path trajectories and propose a novel road-aware masking strategy.
elements.sent-2411-053: In particular, we split a path trajectory into the key path and the mask path, where the key path contains most of the semantic information of a trajectory to assist in the final trajectory representation.
elements.sent-2411-054: Then, we design a spatial-temporal-user joint embedding scheme to fuse spatial, temporal, and user information of road paths for model inputs.
elements.sent-2411-055: We also co-encode the time and segment type to capture periodic information of travel.
elements.sent-2411-056: To train RED, we utilize two training tasks, including next segment prediction and trajectory reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-057: Next segment prediction predicts the next road segment from previous key paths of a trajectory, while trajectory reconstruction reconstructs the original trajectory using the learned representations of the key paths and mask paths.
elements.sent-2411-058: Both tasks exploit the travel information in the trajectory representations.
elements.sent-2411-059: Moreover, we also introduce two strategies to improve trajectory modeling: virtual tokens and a spatial-temporal correlation enhanced attention module.
elements.sent-2411-060: Virtual tokens solve the path misalignment issues when using the Transformer for trajectories, and the attention module enhances Transformer attention to extract the spatial-temporal information of trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-061: To evaluate RED, we conduct experiments on 3 real-world datasets and compare with 9 state-of-the-art TRL methods, includes supervised and self-supervised methods, as well as 7 non-learning trajectory similarity computation methods.
elements.sent-2411-062: We consider 4 popular downstream tasks: travel time estimation, trajectory classification, trajectory similarity computation, and most similar trajectory retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-063: The results show that RED outperforms all existing methods in terms of accuracy across the tasks and datasets.
elements.sent-2411-064: In particular, compared with the best-performing baseline, the average accuracy improvement of RED at travel time estimation, trajectory classification, and trajectory similarity computation are 7.03\%, 12.11\%, and 20.02\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-065: An ablation study suggests that all the types of trajectory information we utilize contribute to the accuracy of TRL and that our model designs are effective.
elements.sent-2411-066: To summarize, we make three main contributions.
elements.sent-2411-067: We identify design limitations of existing TRL methods, including the use of random masking, insufficient utilization of trajectory information, and limited supervision signals for model training.
elements.sent-2411-068: We propose RED as a more effective self-supervised learning framework for TRL.
elements.sent-2411-069: RED features a road-aware mask, spatial-temporal-user embedding, and dual-objective learning to address the limitations of existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-070: We report on an extensive evaluation and comparison of RED with state-of-the-art TRL methods.
elements.sent-2411-071: The results show that RED is capable of higher accuracy than the existing methods across important downstream tasks.

elements.sent-2411-124: We proceed to detail the key innovations of RED, which includes the road-aware masking strategy, the spatial-temporal-user joint embedding, the dual-objective task learning, and the enhanced trajectory modeling techniques.
elements.sent-2411-125: Mask-based self-supervised learning is popular for TRL, which first masks some road segments of a path trajectory, and then learns to reconstruct the masked road segments.
elements.sent-2411-126: However, existing methods use the random masking strategy, which has two problems.
elements.sent-2411-127: First, it may discard essential road segments of a trajectory, leading to some key information loss and inaccurate trajectory representations.
elements.sent-2411-128: Second, the masking ratio requires extensive tuning, and one masking ratio may not suit different trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-129: We introduce a road-aware masking strategy that avoids the two problems.
elements.sent-2411-130: To motivate the road-aware masking strategy, we illustrate the sampling statistics of real-world trajectories in Figure~\ref{fig:statistics1 .}
elements.sent-2411-131: The dataset is Porto, and the sampling interval is about 15 seconds for consecutive points in a trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-132: Since the average length of the road segments in the road network is about 85 meters, we call road segments below 85 meters as short segments and the converse long segments.
elements.sent-2411-133: Figure~\ref{fig:statistics1 (a) shows that the distribution of the sampling points over the road segments is highly skewed, i.e., some segments have many sampling points while some segments have only a few sampling points.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Intuitively, segments with many sampling points are important because they are passed by many trajectories and may take an important role in the road network (e.g., connecting two important areas).
elements.sent-2411-135: Figure~\ref{fig:statistics1 (b) shows that there are more short road segments than long road segments in the road network but more sampling points reside on the long road segments than the short segments.}
elements.sent-2411-136: This suggests that the long segments are more important than the short segments because the trajectories spend more time on them.
elements.sent-2411-137: Overall, Figure~\ref{fig:statistics1 suggests that the road segments are different in their importance.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Thus, by treating all road segments equally, random masking may lose important segments and hinder model training.
elements.sent-2411-139: Driven by the observations from Figure~\ref{fig:statistics1 , our road-aware masking strategy treats road segments with many sampling points and long road segments as key paths, which will not be masked.}

elements.sent-2411-150: The road segments of a city form a complex structure, which can be captured by the road network graph $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-151: We observe that the properties of a segment are affected by itself and its adjacent segments.
elements.sent-2411-152: Therefore, we apply a graph neural network (GNN) to learn embeddings for road segments based on the road network graph $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-153: In particular, GNN computes an embedding for each node in the graph by aggregating the embeddings of its neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-154: We choose the Graph Attention Network (GAT)~\cite{gat , which adopts an attention mechanism for neighbor aggregation.}
elements.sent-2411-155: We feed multiple attributes of a segment as the initial input feature of GAT, including the maximum speed limit, average travel time, segment direction, out-degree, in-degree, segment length, and segment type.
elements.sent-2411-156: To be specific, for the maximum speed limit, average travel time, segment direction, and segment length, we apply the min-max normalization.
elements.sent-2411-157: Regarding segment type, we categorize each segment into eight classes, i.e., [living street, motorway, primary, residential, secondary, tertiary, trunk, unclassified], and we employ one-hot encoding.
elements.sent-2411-158: All these attributes are concatenated to obtain the input features $f_i$ for segment $v_i$.

elements.sent-2411-175: The path encoder is designed to capture key information of a path trajectory and to predict the next segment for learning local information of the trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-176: Section~\ref{sec:road-aware mask shows that the key paths of a trajectory carry much more information than the mask paths, suggesting that a trajectory can be described by the key paths.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Thus, we require the path encoder to learn information from the key paths.
elements.sent-2411-178: Formally, given the key path set of a trajectory $\mathcal{T ^{k = \langle v_1^k, v_2^k, ..., v_{\vert \mathcal{T ^{k \vert ^k \rangle$, the corresponding key path embedding $\mathbf{X ^{k = \langle \mathbf{x _1^k, \mathbf{x _2^k, ..., \mathbf{x _{\vert \mathcal{T ^{k \vert ^k \rangle$ is first generated by the spatial-temporal-user joint embedding, then position encoding~\cite{transformer is added to $\mathbf{X ^{k}$ to introduce the position information of each segment in the path trajectory.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: We feed $\mathbf{X ^{k}$ to the Transformer of the encoder and denote $\widehat{\mathbf{X ^k = Transformer(\mathbf{X ^{k )$ as the output.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: To extract the prediction for the next segment from encoder output, we use a fully connected layer $\mathbf{Y ^k = FC (\widehat{\mathbf{X ^k)$, where $\mathbf{Y ^k \in \mathbb{R ^{\vert \mathcal{T ^k \vert \times \vert \mathcal{V \vert}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Each row of $\mathbf{Y ^k$ is the predicted distribution of a key segment, and each column of $\mathbf{Y ^k$ corresponds to one segment (with $\vert \mathcal{V \vert$ being the number of all segments).}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Then we use cross-entropy loss to compute errors between the predicted value and the ground-truth segment ID for the next segment prediction task as follows. \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}^{nsp}_{\mathcal{T}^k} = -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}^k|} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{T}^k} \log \frac{\exp (\mathbf{Y}_{v_i}^k)}{\sum_{v_j \in \mathcal{V}} \exp (\mathbf{Y}_{v_j}^k)},\end{equation} We average the above loss over the trajectories in a mini-batch to obtain the final next segment prediction loss $\mathcal{L ^{nsp}$.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Note that we predict the next key path in the encoder, so we use causal self-attention to avoid information leakage.. \stitle{Path Decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-184: The path decoder is designed to restore a raw path trajectory by decoding the embeddings of its segments generated by the encoder.
elements.sent-2411-185: However, the path encoder only encodes the key paths and ignores the mask paths.
elements.sent-2411-186: Although the mask paths carry less information about the trajectory, they are indispensable when restoring a complete trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-187: Thus, we use another Transformer model as the decoder to combine the key paths and the mask paths.
elements.sent-2411-188: We first transform all mask path segments to learnable embedding $\mathbf{X ^{m}$, and combine $\mathbf{X ^{m}$ with the outputs of the path encoder for the key paths.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Then we conduct an unshuffle operation to restore the mask path embeddings to their original positions in path trajectory $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-190: This process can be formulated as $\mathbf{X ^{\mathcal{T = Unshuffle(Combine(\widehat{\mathbf{X ^k, \mathbf{X ^{m ))$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: The decoder takes $\mathbf{X ^{\mathcal{T }$ as input and computes hidden embedding output as $\widehat{\mathbf{X ^{\mathcal{T = Transformer(\mathbf{X ^{\mathcal{T )$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: To restore the raw information of trajectory $\mathcal{T}$, we design a trajectory reconstruction task that aims to reconstruct the whole path trajectory from the decoder output $\widehat{\mathbf{X ^{\mathcal{T }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: To achieve this, we first use a fully connected layer to obtain the predictions $\mathbf{Y ^{\mathcal{T = FC(\widehat{\mathbf{X ^{\mathcal{T )$, where $\mathbf{Y ^{\mathcal{T \in \mathbb{R ^{\vert \mathcal{T \vert \times \vert \mathcal{V \vert}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Each row of $\mathbf{Y ^{\mathcal{T }$ is the predicted distribution of a segment of $\mathcal{T}$, and each column of $\mathbf{Y ^{\mathcal{T }$ corresponds to a segment.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Then we use cross-entropy loss for a complete trajectory $\mathcal{T}$ as follows. \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}^{tr}_{\mathcal{T}} = -\frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}|} \sum_{v_i \in \mathcal{T}} \log \frac{\exp (\mathbf{Y}_{v_i}^{\mathcal{T}})}{\sum_{v_j \in \mathcal{V}} \exp (\mathbf{Y}^{\mathcal{T}}_{v_j})},\end{equation} We also average the above loss over all trajectories in a mini-batch to obtain the final trajectory reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L ^{tr}$.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Existing TRL methods consider limited supervised information in the training task, e.g., START~\cite{jiang2023start only reconstructs mask segments of a path trajectory, and Traj2vec~\cite{traj2vec recovers trajectory points.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: RED with dual-objective task explores more supervised information and thus can improve the accuracy of trajectory representations.
elements.sent-2411-198: To use Transformer as the model for the path encoder and decoder, two issues need to be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-199: First, the next segment prediction task is different from next token prediction of language in subtle ways, and directly using Transformer will encounter errors in the input and output (which we call segment misalignment).
elements.sent-2411-200: Second, the Transformer relies on the self-attention among the token embeddings while trajectories contain spatial-temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-201: To utilize such spatial-temporal information, we need to integrate it into Transformer attention.
elements.sent-2411-202: To fix segment misalignment, we introduce virtual tokens for the encoder.
elements.sent-2411-203: To utilize spatial-temporal information of trajectories, we design a time-distance enhanced attention to enhance the Transformer for the decoder.
elements.sent-2411-204: Figure~\ref{fig:attention gives an overview of the enhanced path encoder and decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-205: The next segment prediction task of path encoder can encounter segment misalignment.
elements.sent-2411-206: For instance, given path trajectory $\langle v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5 \rangle$, the key path is $\langle v_1, v_3, v_5 \rangle$ and the mask path is $\langle v_2, v_4 \rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-207: To predict the next key segments, the input of the path encoder is $\langle v_1, v_3 \rangle$, and the expected output of the path encoder is $\langle v_3, v_5 \rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-208: Therefore, the last key segment $v_5$ is not used as input and the first key segment $v_1$ is not used as output.
elements.sent-2411-209: When we combine the output of the encoder with the mask path $\langle v_2, v_4 \rangle$, we will get an incomplete trajectory input $\langle v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5 \rangle$ (i.e., missing $v_1$) for the decoder, which causes incomplete trajectory reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-210: To solve these problems, we introduce three virtual tokens, i.e., a start token, an end token, and an extract token.. \stitle{Start Token.}
elements.sent-2411-211: To complete the first segment in the path encoder output, we introduce a start token [START] at the initiation of all trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-212: However, utilizing [START] to predict the first key segment of a path trajectory cannot consider the topology of the road network.
elements.sent-2411-213: To address this problem, we add [START] as a virtual node in the road network graph $\mathcal{G}$.
elements.sent-2411-214: Specifically, the [START] node is connected to all the nodes of $\mathcal{G}$ with edges, and this augmented graph is also used to generate the spatial encoding.. \stitle{End Token.}
elements.sent-2411-215: The end token [END] serves as the expected output of the path encoder when taking all key segments of a trajectory as input to predict the next segment.. \stitle{Extract Token.}
elements.sent-2411-216: To extract the overall information of a trajectory, we introduce an extract token [EXTRACT] at the end of each trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-217: The encoder output embedding for [EXTRACT] is used as the trajectory representation.
elements.sent-2411-218: To illustrate the virtual tokens, we take the same path trajectory $\langle v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5 \rangle$ as an example.
elements.sent-2411-219: Still, the key path is $\langle v_1, v_3, v_5 \rangle$ and the mask path is $\langle v_2, v_4 \rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-220: By adding virtual tokens, the input of the path encoder becomes $\langle[START], v_1, v_3, v_5, [EXTRACT] \rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-221: The output of the path encoder is to predict $\langle v_1, v_3, v_5, [END] \rangle$, and the output of [EXTRACT] is the trajectory representation.
elements.sent-2411-222: Therefore, the input and output of the path encoder is complete.
elements.sent-2411-223: With spatial-temporal-user joint embedding, a path trajectory $\mathcal{T = \langle \tau_1, \tau_2, ..., \tau_{|\mathcal{T | \rangle$ is transformed to embedding $\mathbf{X = \langle \mathbf{x _1, \mathbf{x _2, ..., \mathbf{x _{|\mathcal{T | \rangle \in \mathbb{R ^{\vert \mathcal{T \vert \times l $.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Then we can use the vanilla Transformer to model trajectory representation.
elements.sent-2411-225: However, directly applying Equation~\ref{eq:vanilla_sa neglects the essential spatial-temporal information in trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Spatially, a path trajectory consists of continuous road segments, and each segment is related most closely to its adjacent segments.
elements.sent-2411-227: Consequently, two segments that are distant in a path trajectory should have small attention scores, while two segments that are close should have large attention scores.
elements.sent-2411-228: Temporally, the sampling timestamps of the segments of a trajectory are consecutive.
elements.sent-2411-229: Two segments with a large sampling time difference should have small attention scores, while the converse is true for two segments with a small sampling time difference.
elements.sent-2411-230: To ensure these properties, we introduce the time-distance enhanced attention to consider the time interval and distance interval between the road segments in a trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-231: In particular, we construct a time-distance attention matrix $\mathbf{A ^{td}$ to incorporate spatial-temporal information to the vanilla Transformer as follows. \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}^\prime = softmax ( \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^{td} ) V, \label{eq:sa}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{A ^{td = (1 - \lambda_2) \mathbf{A ^t + \lambda_2 \mathbf{A ^d$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Follow by START~\cite{jiang2023start , consider a path trajectory $\mathcal{T = \langle \tau_1, \tau_2, ..., \tau_{|\mathcal{T | \rangle$, where $\tau_i = (v_i, t_i)$ denotes the segment $v_i$ and timestamp $t_i$ that the trajectory passes through.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: We first construct a time interval matrix $M^t \in \mathbb{R ^{\vert \mathcal{T \vert \times \vert \mathcal{T \vert}$, where element $m_{i,j ^t \in M^t$ is the difference $\vert t_i - t_j \vert$ between the sampling timestamps of segments $v_i$ and $v_j$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: Then, we introduce a transformation $f(m_{i,j ^t) = \frac{1 {\log(e + g_t(m_{i,j ^t))}$ to make larger values smaller to capture correlation based on time interval, where $g_t(m_{i,j ^t) = \frac{m_{i,j ^t {60}$ maps seconds to minutes to avoid excessive time differences between the segments.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-235: In the path encoder, the inputs are key paths of a trajectory, so the time interval matrix is a sub-matrix of $M^t$.
elements.sent-2411-236: To incorporate the information of $M^t$ into the trajectory representation, we use a fully connected layers to transform each element $m_{i,j ^t \in M^t$ to a vector in the embedding space as $\delta_{i,j ^t = FC(m_{i,j ^t)$, where the $\delta_{i,j ^t \in \mathbb{R ^{\frac{l {2 }$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-237: Consequently, we obtain the time interval hidden embedding $\Delta^t \in \mathbb{R ^{\vert \mathcal{T \vert \times \vert \mathcal{T \vert \times \frac{l {2 }$ for $\mathcal{T}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-238: Then, we use another fully connected layer to transform $\Delta^t$ to time interval correlation matrix $\mathbf{A ^t \in \mathbb{R ^{\vert \mathcal{T \vert \times \vert \mathcal{T \vert}$, where each element $\alpha^t_{i,j \in \mathbf{A ^t$ is a time interval correlation value, and $\alpha^t_{i,j = FC(\delta_{i,j ^t)$.. \stitle{Distance Interval Correlation.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: Like time interval correlation, we first construct a travel distance interval matrix $M^d \in \mathbb{R ^{\vert \mathcal{T \vert \times \vert \mathcal{T \vert}$ for a path trajectory $\mathcal{T}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: In particular, we use the travel length between two segments as $m_{i,j ^d \in M^d$.}

elements.sent-2411-242: We also use a fully connected layer to map the transformed distances to the final distance interval correlation matrix $\mathbf{A ^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-243: The training time of RED encompasses three terms: i.e., road-aware masking, graph neural network, and trajectory encoding.
elements.sent-2411-244: The road-aware masking has time complexity $\mathcal{O (\vert \mathcal{D \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{T \vert)$, where $\vert \mathcal{T \vert$ is the average trajectory length and $\vert \mathcal{D \vert$ is the cardinality of the trajectory dataset.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: The GNN training has complexity $\mathcal{O (\vert \mathcal{V \vert \cdot l + \vert \mathcal{E \vert \cdot l )$, where $l$ is the embedding dimensionality, $\vert \mathcal{V \vert$ and $\vert \mathcal{E \vert$ are the numbers of nodes and edges in the road network.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-246: The complexity of encoding the trajectories is $\mathcal{O (\vert \mathcal{D \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{T ^k \vert^2 \cdot l \cdot L_e + \vert \mathcal{D \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{T \vert^2 \cdot l \cdot L_d)$, where $\vert \mathcal{T ^k \vert$ in the number of key segments in trajectory $\mathcal{T}$ and $\vert \mathcal{T ^k \vert < \vert \mathcal{T \vert$, $L_e$ and $L_d$ are the numbers of path encoder and path decoder layers.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: To conduct model inference for a trajectory dataset $\mathcal{D '$, we use the path encoder to compute the vector representations.}
elements.sent-2411-248: The complexity of this is $\mathcal{O (\vert \mathcal{D ' \vert \vert \mathcal{T \vert^2 \cdot l \cdot L_e)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-249: The resulting trajectory representations take $\mathcal{O (l \cdot \vert \mathcal{D ' \vert)$ space.}}
elements.sent-2411-250: Using the representations, computing the similarity of two trajectories has time cost $\mathcal{O (l)$.}
elements.sent-2411-251: In contrast, traditional trajectory similarity algorithms (e.g., DTW, EDR) have cost $\mathcal{O (\vert \mathcal{D \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{T \vert^2)$ because they use dynamic programming.. \stitle{Limitations.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: RED targets trajectories on the road network (i.e., involving map matching and road segments) and needs to be adjusted for other types of trajectories (e.g., POI trajectories, animal and human flow trajectories).
elements.sent-2411-253: Moreover, RED focuses on trajectory-based tasks (e.g., similarity computation and travel time estimation) and is unsuitable for road network related tasks that do not work at the trajectory level, e.g., road classification and flow estimation.

elements.sent-2411-266: We use a machine running Ubuntu 20.04 and with an NVIDIA RTX GeForce 4090 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-267: We set the embedding size $l$ to 128 in all methods, the number of GAT layers to 3, and the number of encoder and decoder layers to 6.

elements.sent-2411-270: We set $\lambda_1 = 0.1$ for the dual-objective tasks, and $\lambda_2 = 0.5$ for time-distance correlation.
elements.sent-2411-271: We pre-train RED using the AdamW~\cite{adamw optimizer.}
elements.sent-2411-272: The batch sizes are 64, 32, and 32, the training epochs are 10, 30, and 5, and the learning rates are 1e-4 for Porto and Chengdu, 2e-4 for Rome.. \stitle{Downstream Task Settings.}
elements.sent-2411-273: For all downstream tasks, we only use the encoder of RED and use complete trajectories as input.
elements.sent-2411-274: For trajectory classification and travel-time estimation, we use AdamW~\cite{adamw to fine-tune the encoder.}
elements.sent-2411-275: The batch size is 64, the training epoch is 30, and the learning rate is 1e-4.
elements.sent-2411-276: For trajectory classification on Porto and Rome, we classify trajectories according to user IDs and remove the user encoding to avoid information leakage during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-277: For Chengdu, we classify trajectories based on whether the vehicle is carrying passengers and keep the user encoding during fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-278: We add an extra linear layer to get predictions and use cross-entropy as the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-279: For travel time estimation, we remove all time information, including the time encoding as well as the time correlation, except for the departure time, to avoid time information leakage.
elements.sent-2411-280: We also add an extra linear layer to predict travel time and use mean square error (MSE) as the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-281: The travel times of all methods are reported in minutes.
elements.sent-2411-282: For trajectory similarity computation, we conduct two types of experiments without fine-tuning, including trajectory similarity computation and most similar trajectory retrieval, and we use the inner-product of trajectory vectors as the similarity score.. \stitle{Performance Metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-283: For travel time estimation, we report the mean absolute error (MAE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and root mean square error (RMSE).
elements.sent-2411-284: For trajectory multi-classifica\-tion, we report the Micro-F1 (Mi-F1), Macro-F1 (Ma-F1), and Recall@5 values.
elements.sent-2411-285: For the trajectory binary-classification, we report the F1-score, accuracy, and precision.
elements.sent-2411-286: For trajectory similarity tasks, we use Hit Ratio i.e., the HR@1, HR@5, HR@10 values, for trajectory similarity computation and the mean rank (MR) for most similar trajectory retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-287: Ideally, the MR value should be equal to 1.

elements.sent-2411-380: Trajectory analysis is fundamental to many spatial-temporal analysis tasks and is an important aspect of urban data mining and analytics.
elements.sent-2411-381: Many methods have been proposed to learn embeddings of trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-382: For example, T2vec~\cite{t2vec , Traj2SimVec~\cite{traj2simvec , GTS~\cite{gts , TrajGAT~\cite{trajgat , and GRLSTM~\cite{grlstm learn trajectory representations for similarity computation.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-383: Traj2vec~\cite{traj2vec and E$^2$DTC~\cite{e2dtc use the seq2seq model for trajectory clustering.}}
elements.sent-2411-384: Further, GM-VSAE~\cite{gm-vsae and RL4OASD~\cite{oasd target anomalous trajectory detection.}}
elements.sent-2411-385: These methods learn trajectory representations for specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-386: In contrast, our RED considers generic trajectory representation learning for diverse downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-387: Existing generic TRL methods can be classified into RNN-based and Transformer-based methods.. \stitle{RNN-based Methods.}
elements.sent-2411-388: Trembr~\cite{trembr uses an RNN-based encoder-decoder and uses the timestamp of each road segment to learn temporal information, but it fails to capture periodic behaviors of different road types in trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-389: PIM~\cite{pim first uses node2vec~\cite{node2vec to learn road segment embeddings and then conducts self-supervised RNN-based mutual information maximization to learn trajectory representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-390: WSCCL~\cite{yang2022wsccl proposes a weakly supervised contrastive learning method with curriculum learning using LSTMs~\cite{lstm .}}
elements.sent-2411-391: HMTRL+~\cite{HMTRL+ uses GRU to model the spatial-temporal correlation of trajectories.}
elements.sent-2411-392: These RNN-based methods exhibit long training times, struggle to handle long trajectories and achieve much lower accuracy than Transformer-based methods.. \stitle{Transformer-based Methods.}
elements.sent-2411-393: Since trajectories are sequential structures, that fit very well with the Transformer, transformer-based methods have become the main focus of the TRL research.
elements.sent-2411-394: Toast~\cite{toast proposes a traffic context aware skip-gram~\cite{skip-gram module and a trajectory-enhanced Transformer module to learn trajectory representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-395: JCLRNT~\cite{mao2022JCLRNT offers domain-specific augmentations for road-road contrast and trajectory-trajectory contrast to incorporate valuable inter-relations.}
elements.sent-2411-396: START~\cite{jiang2023start incorporates temporal regularities and travel semantics into generic TRL by random masking strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-397: START also features data augmentation techniques involving trajectory trimming, temporal shifting, and contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-398: LightPath~\cite{lightpath uses different mask ratios to augment trajectories and then applies a teacher-student contrastive model to learn trajectory representations.}
elements.sent-2411-399: However, these approaches either ignore spatial-temporal information (e.g., LightPath, Toast) or rely heavily on contrastive learning for data augmentation (e.g., JCLRNT, START, LightPath).
elements.sent-2411-400: In particular, the same data augmentation technique generally does not work well across different datasets, and also doubles the training dataset and increases the training time.
elements.sent-2411-401: In contrast, RED considers the comprehensive information of the trajectories and does not require data augmentation technologies.
elements.sent-2411-402: We propose RED, a framework that utilizes self-supervised learning for trajectory representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-403: Motivated by the limitations of existing studies, RED aims to utilize comprehensive information in trajectory, including road, user, spatial, temporal, travel, and movement.
elements.sent-2411-404: For such purpose, RED features three key designs: road-aware masking strategy, spatial-temporal-user joint embedding, and dual-objective task learning.
elements.sent-2411-405: Experimental results show that RED consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy across different datasets and downstream tasks, thus advancing the state-of-the-art in trajectory representation learning.
